quality.rationale: Requires relativistic transformation of EM field tensor combined with superposition principle; multi-step logical chain connecting current, charge density, and frame-dependent fields.

origin.question:
Problem 4.1. Find the magnetic field $\underline{\mathsf{B}}$ from a current I in an infinitely long straight wire, by appropriate Lorentz transformations and superpositions of the electric field of an infinitely long straight charge distribution.

## Solution

Solution 4.1. Suppose the wire lies on the $z$ -axis of cylindrical polar coordinates and has proper charge density $ho_0$ . In the rest frame of the wire $\mathbf{J}^0 = ho_0$ and $\underline{\mathbf{J}} = 0$ , and from Gauss' law the only nonvanishing component of the field tensor is $\mathbf{E}^{\mathrm{r}} = ho_0\mathbf{A} / 2\pi \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{F}^{0\mathrm{r}}$ , where $\mathbf{A}$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. If the wire is moving in the $+z$ direction with velocity $ eta$ relative to the lab, the Lorentz transformation $\Lambda_{0}^{0'} = \gamma$ , $\Lambda_{z}^{0'} =  eta y$ gives
$$
J^{0^{\prime}} = \gamma ho_{0} \quad J^{z^{\prime}} =  eta \gamma ho_{0}
$$
$$
\mathbf {B}^{\hat {\phi}} = \mathbf {F}^{z^{\prime} \mathbf {r}^{\prime}} =  oldsymbol {\Lambda}_{^{\prime} 0}^{z^{\prime}} \mathbf {F}^{0 \mathbf {r}} =  eta \gamma ho_{0} \mathbf {A} / 2 \pi \mathbf {r} \qquad \mathbf {E}^{\mathbf {r}^{\prime}} = \gamma ho_{0} \mathbf {A} / 2 \pi \mathbf {r} .
$$
$(\mathbf{B}^{\hat{\phi}}$ is the "physical component," i.e. the component related to the unit vector $\mathbf{e}_{\phi}$ .) If we now superpose the field of a similar wire of charge density $-\gamma ho_{0}$ , at rest in the lab, the total charge density and $\underline{\mathbf{E}}$ field both cancel. The current $\mathrm{I} = \int \mathrm {J} ^ { \mathbf {z } } \mathrm {d } \mathbf {x } \mathrm {d } \mathbf {y } =  eta \gamma ho _ {0} \mathbf {A }$ produces only a B field $\mathbf {B } ^ { \hat { \phi } } = \mathrm {I } / 2 \pi \mathrm {r }$ .
origin.solution:
Suppose the wire lies on the $z$ -axis of cylindrical polar coordinates and has proper charge density $ho_0$ . In the rest frame of the wire $\mathbf{J}^0 = ho_0$ and $\underline{\mathbf{J}} = 0$ , and from Gauss' law the only nonvanishing component of the field tensor is $\mathbf{E}^{\mathrm{r}} = ho_0\mathbf{A} / 2\pi \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{F}^{0\mathrm{r}}$ , where $\mathbf{A}$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. If the wire is moving in the $+z$ direction with velocity $ eta$ relative to the lab, the Lorentz transformation $\Lambda_{0}^{0'} = \gamma$ , $\Lambda_{z}^{0'} =  eta y$ gives
$$
J^{0^{\prime}} = \gamma ho_{0} \quad J^{z^{\prime}} =  eta \gamma ho_{0}
$$
$$
\mathbf {B}^{\hat {\phi}} = \mathbf {F}^{z^{\prime} \mathbf {r}^{\prime}} =  oldsymbol {\Lambda}_{^{\prime} 0}^{z^{\prime}} \mathbf {F}^{0 \mathbf {r}} =  eta \gamma ho_{0} \mathbf {A} / 2 \pi \mathbf {r} \qquad \mathbf {E}^{\mathbf {r}^{\prime}} = \gamma ho_{0} \mathbf {A} / 2 \pi \mathbf {r} .
$$
$(\mathbf{B}^{\hat{\phi}}$ is the "physical component," i.e. the component related to the unit vector $\mathbf{e}_{\phi}$ .) If we now superpose the field of a similar wire of charge density $-\gamma ho_{0}$ , at rest in the lab, the total charge density and $\underline{\mathbf{E}}$ field both cancel. The current $\mathrm{I} = \int \mathrm {J} ^ { \mathbf {z } } \mathrm {d } \mathbf {x } \mathrm {d } \mathbf {y } =  eta \gamma ho _ {0} \mathbf {A }$ produces only a B field $\mathbf {B } ^ { \hat { \phi } } = \mathrm {I } / 2 \pi \mathrm {r }$ .
